title: GradientEntry installed without validating seq_num freshness
description:
File: python/src/lichen/loadng/discovery.py
Lines: 150-160

When processing an RREP, a GradientEntry is installed unconditionally:
```python
self.gradient.update(
    GradientEntry(
        destination=rrep.originator,
        next_hop=from_neighbor,
        hop_count=install_hops,
        seq_num=rrep.seq_num,
        source=GradientSource.RREP,
        expires=now + GRADIENT_TIMEOUT_MS,
    ),
    now=now,
)
```

Problem: The code does not check if an existing gradient entry has a higher (more recent) sequence number before overwriting. Per LOADng spec, sequence numbers should be compared to ensure only fresher routes are installed.

Impact:
1. A delayed RREP with an old seq_num can overwrite a fresher route
2. An attacker could inject RREPs with low seq_nums to downgrade routes
3. Out-of-order RREP processing leads to suboptimal routing

Note: The GradientTable.update() method may perform this check internally. If so, this is not a bug. However, the discovery layer should not rely on implicit behavior.

Fix: Check seq_num before updating:
```python
existing = self.gradient.lookup(rrep.originator, now)
if existing is not None:
    # Only update if new seq_num is greater (with wrap handling)
    if not _is_seq_newer(rrep.seq_num, existing.seq_num):
        pass  # Skip stale update
        return RrepResult(...)  # Or handle appropriately
```

Where _is_seq_newer handles 16-bit sequence number wrap-around comparison.
